title: A New Algorithmic Identity: Soft Biopolitics and the Modulation of Control
author: Cheney-Lippold

opening_move.description: Online marketing algorithms infer categories of identity (gender, class, race) from browsing behaviour without reference to self-declaration or biology, creating a new form of identity-formation that exceeds existing Foucauldian frameworks of biopolitics.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: This new 'algorithmic identity' requires supplementing Foucauldian biopolitics with the concept of 'soft biopolitics' — a mode of control that operates at the level of categories rather than individual bodies, and that continuously redefines categories through cybernetic feedback.

theoretical_positioning.description: The paper extends Foucault's biopolitics with the concept of 'soft biopolitics' to account for the cybernetic, modulating character of algorithmic categorization, supplemented by Deleuzian control theory.

epistemic_mode.interpretive_methods: Theoretical-interpretive analysis of algorithmic categorization practices using philosophical concepts (biopolitics, control, code); uses hypothetical and illustrative examples (gendered ad-targeting) rather than empirical data.
epistemic_mode.notes: The 'empirical' content consists of illustrative hypotheticals and generic descriptions of algorithmic marketing practices, not original research. The argument is fundamentally conceptual.

case_type.case_name: Online algorithmic identity inference (web analytics, gendered ad-targeting)

confidence_notes: This is a theoretical paper applying and extending Foucauldian biopolitics to algorithmic identity. The 'cases' (gendered ad-targeting, web browsing scenarios) are illustrative hypotheticals, not empirical research. The normative claim — soft biopolitics extends control in new ways that liberal politics cannot easily contest — is implicit but strong.
